CURRENT AFFAIRS.
The attempt of the Development Comm
bolster up Plunkett House and the Hairy Fairies is yc
another instance of annoying English interference in
Trish affaivs. The I.A.0.S. have the ear of certain
people in England just as Pensioner Yeats was ubl(’a’ to
get a similar English ear for the notorious *“ Playboy"’—
which, by the way, the astute Mr. Martin Harvey
dropped like a hot potato. The clique that ran the
‘“ Playhoy " are somewhat similar to those who run
Plunkett. In England the Cromwellian with a bl"(‘)guc
is greedily accepted: as an ‘' educated”’ and “en-
lightened *’ Trishman, and so a clique find it easy enough
to run Plunkett and the ‘‘ Playboy’ in England as
- genuine Trish goods. ~ Bvidently the Development Com-
missioners, with characteristic Inglish assumption,
think that the people who are up against the I.A.O.S.
are poor ignorant people who ought to be thwarted for
their own good by superior Tinglish enlightenment. The
~ replies which the Development Commissioners received
or will receive from the men whom they tried to hook,

will probably be startling news to these awkward med-
dlers in Irish affairs. The attempt to bolster up the
I.A.0.8. may now, it may be expected, be given up as
a hopeless job.

But what is to become of co-operation in Ireland ?
It is not because the I.A.0.S. has failed and is so much
opposed by popular opinion in Ireland that no public
effort, aided by funds from the Development grant,
should be made. The I.A.O.S. is not co-operation in
Ireland, though some of its fairies may think that it is.
Co-operation applied to the Irish farming industry is
very much needed, and a genuine businesslike scheme
of co-operation in Ireland would command great back-
ing and support. It is not co-operation that is suspect
in Ireland, it is merely the I.A.O.S. There is a future
of useful work before a genuine agricultural co-opera-
tive movement. Co-operation in marketing goods, par-
ticularly the produce of small farms and plots, is, for
instance, a much needed development in Irish rural
life. There are many co-operators who would not touch
the I.A.0.8., who are yet enthusiastic in their desire
to sec co-operation developed in Ireland. Is it not
possible for such people to make a new and clean start ?
We would like to hear from readers who are anxious
that the cause of co-operation should be pushed on in
TIreland.

Lord Ardilaun runs an obscure daily paper called the
“ Express.”” We understand it is read by bigotted
parsons and other bigots, but it is a very obscure paper.
From year’s end to year’s end we, like most other
people, never, or scarcely ever, see a copy of it, bub
for a certain reason we purchased a copy of the issue
of September 11th. If we were not the only Home
Rulers that saw that particular issue, certainly very
few other Home Rulers saw it. Our eye caught an
advertisement from the Great Sourface Railway—
that’s one way the shareholder’s money is squandered.
And what do you think the advertisement was about ?
Nothing other than the special passenger train to
Nenagh in connection with the Home Rule demon-
stration in that town that was held last Sunday! It
is & wonder the Great Sourface people did not adver-
tise this Home Rule special train in the TLondon
‘“ Globe,” ‘‘ Morning Post,” and ‘‘ Times.” How
many Home Rulers read Ardilaun’s *‘ Express '’ ? Bub
when the Great Sourface has money to spend of course
Ardilaun must get his share, and so the shareholders’
money was thrown away.

Scene—Near Plunkett House.
(Inter Healy of the ‘‘ Irish Times."’)

Healy.—What’s that ?
Methought I heard the eracking of a whip,
Or do my nerves deceive me. Ever since
That most pernicious LEADER did refer
Unto my treatment of the Viceroy’s wife,
As most unworthy of a proper man,
My carcass trembles at the very thought =
Of horsewhips, and each corner of the street
Doth in my nervous apprehension hold
Some chivalrous assailer with a stick,
Or cat-o’-nine-tails in his doughty hand,
To whack me with a vengeance.

(Enter a Hairy Fairy behind.)

H. F. (clapping him on the back).—Thou doughty
ampion of the hairy gang:
Healy.—Hands off, thou ruffian there.

Help ! help! police!

What is the matter!

And not a grim chastiser ?

We have seen that the ‘ Liberal ** Birrell proposes
to give secondary teachers in Ireland only’a small part
of what is due to Ireland, and proposes conditions with
this attenuated grant that the country cannot accept.
Catholic Treland wants religious teachers as wel} as lay
teachers, and it must fight against the assumption that
the State can condition the grant. of the people’s own
money by stipulating that a certain proportion of
teach in each school must be non-clerics. ~ The
country is anxious that everything reasonable should be
done for the lay secondary teachers, but if the *“ Libe-
ral”’ Government thinks it can slip in an obnoxious
principle with its offer of part of our own money it is
mistaken. It is fair enough that a standard of compz-
tency should be fixed, but the country will not have
dictation from the Government as to how many lay
teachers a school must employ v matfer of fact
there are about one-third mo vy teachers in the
Catholic boys’ schools than are required by the pro-
posed Government condition, but the principle involved
in the attempt at fixing any proportion is a sinister
thing to be fought. If a cleric is a competent teacher,
why is he not on the same footing before the law as a
competent lay teacher? The ** Irish Times’’ of cours
has kindly explained that the cleric works for ** super-
natural rewards.”’ Against great cdds many Catholic
schools have been established, and now Birrell wants
to tell the owners of these schools how many clerics
they may employ as teachers!

In the course of the principal speech of Mr. Dillon at
Kells and of Mr. Devlin at Nenagh we did not notice
any ence to the dumping of Norway into the secre-
taryship of the Dublin Post Office. So far as we know
the Irish Party has made no protest against that con-
templated insult and outrage on Treland. Are the Ivish
Party taking it lying down? What of the Publin six ?
We believe Mr. Field made some protest, but then Mr.
Tield makes so many protests that no one minds them
very much. - What of Mr. Nannetti? What of Mr. P.
J. Brady ? Has he nothing to say against this insult
to Treland ? Whilst prominent local bodies have en-
tered their protests the Irish Party appear to be keeping
up a cgnspiracy of silence.

The Belfast blackguards resumed their work on
Saturday. A coulributor in this week’s issue points
out that the stones must have. been deliber-

‘ ately brought into the grounds. This black-

- guardism on the sporting field should be denounced
by every honest man; but the *“ Irish Times " that has
plenty of wrath for Lady Aberdecn, and that has con-
veniently discovered that Catholic clerical teachers
work for ‘‘ supernatural rewards,’’ has had no- editorial
denunciation nor even reference to this latest piece of
Belfast blackguardism in its editorial column on Mon-
day or Tuesday. Some Iinglish Unionist papers are
laying contribution on these Belfast blackguards in
their endeavours to make a point against Home Rule,
and the ‘‘ Irish Times,”’ that has nothing to say in its
editorial column, uses these English Unionist com-
ments in its London correspondence to attempt to make
party capital out of the blackguards in a second-hand
sort of way. The first paragraph in the London corre-
spondence in Arnott’s paper of Tuesday is as follows :—

The serious aspect of the disturbances in Belfast
are not lost sight of here. The Government are
warned that they are a sign that Ulster is in carn-
est, and will not be trifled with. The * Pall
Mall Gazette " states: “ The guilt of Saturday’s
disorder, and of all that may follow, vests upon

- those politicians who have made the future intoler-
able to loyalist minds, and have convinced the best
citizens of Ireland that they must depend on them-
selves to keep anarchy, injustice, and spoliation
from their thresholds.” The *‘ Globe ' says: *“ We
ask the Government in all seriousness to talke note
of the temper of which this outbreak is a proof.
Let our rulers consider the scene in Celtic Park,
and ask themselves what will happen when Home
Rule is a fact, and not merely a menace.”

So this blackguardism is held up as a reason why His
Majesty’s Government should perform the operation of
‘*‘ Hands up ”’ to Belfast miscreants! That is a pretty
attitude for English Conservatives to take.

What is left to be done in face of the dumping of
Norway over here? The ‘‘ Freeman '’ is editorially
cowed; apparently the Irish Party declines to do
anything; the A.0.H., which is pictured for party pur-
poses as such a dreadful engine of tyranny and per-
secution, so far as we know, has not let even a-puff out
of itself against this importation. ~Can the Irish Post
Office staff do nothing? Is a strike impossible? Sup-
posing all the chief clerks laid down their pens, put on
their hats and walked out when Norway took his seat
in the Secretarial chair? Of course, when Mr. Sﬂxngel
sees the Irish Party, and its more or less official daily
organ, taking it easy, and when even the A.O.H. has
not, so far as we know, even passed a resolution, he
probably thinks that the way is clear for landing the
dump with other unnecessarily imported goods on the
quays of Dublin.

In Tuesday’s ‘‘ Freeman,’’ under the heading ““ P.O.,
Secretaryship,” that paper publishes from ‘“a corre-
spondent,’’ not a protest, but a sort of lame and tame
effort to explain the dump. That is the ‘“ Freeman’s’”
latest contribution to the question! It is wonderful
what some people can write and suggest when they
want to explain away something. Only last week we saw
that Arnott’s bitterly anti-Catholic paper, the libeller
of Canon MelInerney, suddenly struck on a rich vein
of virtue in Irish Catholic clergymen; it discovered that
these clergymen in their capacity as teachers worked
for ‘‘ supernatural rewards.”  The correspondent in
the *“ Freeman ” points out that the Secretary of the
Irish Post Office had not power commensurate with his
position and pay. We can well believe that, for when
we had our controversy with Iigerton, that gentleman
had not the power to decide whether or not ‘“ Alf Fox ”
was current matter; he had to seek in London for an
answer to that question. It appears that in 1907 a
Parliamentary Committee recommended an increase in
the powers of the Secretary in Ireland, but that recom-

colours of the same hue. Play proceeded quictly and
well, and the competitors were encouraged by the
cheers of their respective friends. Half time was
reached, and during the pause an electric shock seem-
ingly pervaded the dense mass, for, suddenly, and with-
out a word of warning, the Blues* swooped down on the
surprised Celts, and with large stones (which they must

. have brought with them as the field is cinder strewn),

they sought to beat them off the ground. For a moment
only the Celts endured the unprovoked assault, buf,
when they realized “‘ the totally unprecedented act in
the annals of sport,” as the Blues’ own organ, * ''he

SECONDARY TEACHERS AND PRIMARY
SCHOLARS.

It is a good many years now since I last wrote about
secondary teachers, and I made not a few enemies when
I did. I was taken to task for a phrase I then used that
lay secondary teachers in Ireland were either ‘idiots,
wrecks, or birds of pa re,”” most of all by one, whom I
may congratulate on having happily proved to be a bird
of passage himself, since he now occupies a position of
much greater dignity and emolument than that of second-
ary teacher. I am in no way disposed to retract the
phrase, which seems to me well fitted to express the truth
that anyone, who would adopt the profession of secondary
teacher under existing conditions as his permanent em-
ployment, is an idiot, unless indeed he be already a broken
man. And broken men too often secondary teachers are.
Nothing could be less satisfactory. The analogy to an
assistant in a drapery house is wholly inapplicable, apart
even from the fact that a long apprenticeship and a
severe standard of character is, I believe, commonly
cxacted in that business. The shop assistant who sells
me a tie or a safety razor may be an atheist or a man of
bad moral character ; the quality of his wares will remain
unaffected. But in the case of a school teacher, at least
to any who take the Christian view of education, the
character of the man is of the first importance. . And
you may get many things cheap in this world, but you
must always be prepared to pay for character.

Now secondary teaching is, at present, in a most un-
satisfactory state. Just as the stringent application of
Ricardo principles, operating in a limited field had
reduced Irish farming below the level of efficiency, before
the Land Acts, so a fierce commercial competition among
our schools, has given us cheap but inefficient education,
inefficient at least in the character of the teachers. To
this religious teachers are in large part an exception,
since the honourable nature of their occupation prevents
cheapness in their case from doing its worst. They are,
however, in too many cases imperfectly trained. Educa-
tion is too often the weak point of the religious teacher,
as character is that of the lay teacher.

Now the efforts of any reformer should clearly be
directed to affording an opportunity ol university educa-
tion to the religious, whilst on the other hand making the

MERCITANTS.

The Guardians of the above Union will, on Wed-
nes , the 25th September, 1912, consider Tenders
for the supply for Six Months, from the 1st October,
1912, of Flour, per cwt.; Oatmeal, best patent cut,
per cwt.; Wholemeal, per cw Indian Meal, best
red, per ewt. ; Rice Flour, bakers’, per cwt.

The Wholemeal, Flour, Oatmeal, Indian Meal, and
Rice Flour must be equal to samples, which can be
seen at the Master’s Office. All commodities must
be ground by Irish Mills, a certificate to accompany
each delivery of Flour, Wholemeal, Oatmeal, Indian
Meal, and Rice Flour, stating the name of the Irish Mill
in which said article is ground, and signed by the Con-
tractor and Owner of the Mill.

On acceptance of the Tender, the Contractor shall
at once deliver One Ton of Flour identical to the Work-
house sample, which will be held in store to test all future
deliveries.

The ton so delivered to be renewed once a month.

Contractors will be ‘required to strictly comply
with Articles 7 and 13 of the Union Accounts Order,
1905, which state that the second meeting of each

month shall be the Stated Finance Meeting, and
that accounts (which should be furnished- monthly

on official forms only) must be in the hands of the
Clerk three clear days at least before such Stated Finance
Meeting. '

Contractors will also be bound by Section 51 (7)
of the Local Government (Ireland) Act., 1898, which
provides that goods supplied during any hall-year
must be paid for within three months after the close of
such half-year, and not afterwards.

Intending Contractors arve particularly requested
to read over carefully the Tender and Bond before signing
them.

The half-years end on 30th September and 31st March
respectively.

Sealed Tenders, Printed Forms of which can be
had on application to the Master of the Workhouse,
and none - other will be considered, to be lodged
with me not later than 12 o'clock noon on the 25th Sep-
tember, 1912, on which day they will be opened by the
Board. :

Solvent security will be required for the due perfor-
mance of the Contract, and Tenders, without any altera-
tion, must bear the genuine signature of the persons
tendering and their sureties.

The Guardians do not bind themselve
lowest or any Tender.
